q: A series LCR circuit is connected to an AC source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The circuit contains a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, an inductor of inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, and a capacitor of capacitive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>130</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power factor of circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
question: A series LCR circuit is connected to an AC source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The circuit contains a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, an inductor of inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, and a capacitor of capacitive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>130</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power factor of circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
solution:
Power factor in LCR circuit is given by, 

cos ϕ=RZ, where, Z is the total impedance of the circuit.

So, cosϕ=RR2+XC-XL2

Putting the values, we have

⇒cos ϕ=80(80)2+(60)2

⇒cos ϕ=80100⇒810

Thus, the value of x=8.

subject: Physics
q:
Match the List-I with List-II.

  List I   List II
A AC generator I Presence of both L and C
B Transformer II Electromagnetic Induction
C Resonance phenomenon to occur III Quality factor
D Sharpness of resonance IV Mutual Inductance

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the List-I with List-II.

  List I   List II
A AC generator I Presence of both L and C
B Transformer II Electromagnetic Induction
C Resonance phenomenon to occur III Quality factor
D Sharpness of resonance IV Mutual Inductance

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) An AC generator works on the electromagnetic induction principle. So, A→II

(B) The transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. So, B→IV

(C) Resonance occurs when the value of inductive and capacitive reactance have equal magnitude but a phase difference of 180°.  So, C→I

(D) The sharpness of resonance is defined using the quality factor which explains how fast energy decay in an oscillating system. D→III

subject: Physics
q: A 110 V,50 Hz, AC source is connected in the circuit (as shown in figure). The current through the resistance 55 Ω, at resonance in the circuit, will be _____A.
question: A 110 V,50 Hz, AC source is connected in the circuit (as shown in figure). The current through the resistance 55 Ω, at resonance in the circuit, will be _____A.
solution:
Let current in the inductance is iL, through the capacitor is iC and through resistance is iR at any instance as shown in the figure.

According to the Kirchoff's junction law, iR=iL+iC.

At resonance XC=XL hence iL=iC. Also, current through the capacitor and inductor have phase difference of π rad. Therefore, they will form destructive interference and hence the current through the resistance will be 0.

subject: Physics
q: An a.c. source of angular frequency $\omega$ is connected across a resistor $R$ and a capacitor $C$ in series. The current is observed as $I$. Now the frequency of the source is changed to $\omega/4$, (keeping the voltage unchanged) the current is found to be $I/3$. The ratio of resistance to reactance at frequency $\omega$ is
question: An a.c. source of angular frequency $\omega$ is connected across a resistor $R$ and a capacitor $C$ in series. The current is observed as $I$. Now the frequency of the source is changed to $\omega/4$, (keeping the voltage unchanged) the current is found to be $I/3$. The ratio of resistance to reactance at frequency $\omega$ is
solution:
The current in the RC circuit at frequency $\omega$ is given by

$I = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2}}$

where $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ is the capacitive reactance.

When the frequency is changed to $\omega/4$, the new reactance becomes $X_C' = \dfrac{1}{(\omega/4)C} = 4X_C$.

The new current is given as $I/3$, so

$\dfrac{I}{3} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + (4X_C)^2}}$

Substituting the expression for $I$, we get

$\dfrac{V}{3\sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2}} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + 16X_C^2}}$

Squaring both sides, we obtain

$\dfrac{1}{9(R^2 + X_C^2)} = \dfrac{1}{R^2 + 16X_C^2}$

$9R^2 + 9X_C^2 = R^2 + 16X_C^2$

$8R^2 = 7X_C^2$

$\dfrac{R^2}{X_C^2} = \dfrac{7}{8}$

$\dfrac{R}{X_C} = \sqrt{\dfrac{7}{8}}$

Answer: $\sqrt{\dfrac{7}{8}}$

q: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, power factor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. If a capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub></math> is added to the circuit the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be :
question: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, power factor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. If a capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub></math> is added to the circuit the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be :
solution:
In case of LR circuit:

Given: XL=R

P1=cosϕ=RZ=RR2+XL2=12

In case of LCR circuit:

P2=cosϕ=RZ=RR2+XL-XC2=1

Therefore,

P1P2=1:2

The power dissipated at resonance condition is ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A series LCR circuit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi>F</mi></math> is connected across an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AC</mi></math> supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>V</mi><mo>,</mo></math> having variable frequency. The power dissipated at resonance condition is ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
XL=XC (due to resonance)

Z=R so irms=VZ=VR

V2R=250×2505=125×102 W

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : An AC circuit undergoes electrical resonance if it contains either a capacitor or an inductor.

Statement II: An AC circuit containing a pure capacitor or a pure inductor consumes high power due to its non-zero power factor. In the light of above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : An AC circuit undergoes electrical resonance if it contains either a capacitor or an inductor.

Statement II: An AC circuit containing a pure capacitor or a pure inductor consumes high power due to its non-zero power factor. In the light of above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but statement II is true
Statement I is true but statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
For resonance to occur the condition is XL=XC.

So, both inductor and capacitor is required.

The power factor is given by cosϕ.

For an inductor the power factor is zero-lagging, cos90°=0. The angle between the voltage and current is 90°.

For a capacitor the power factor is zero-leading.

Hence, both statements are false.

q: A telegraph line of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> has a capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and it carries an alternating current at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> kilo cycle per second. If minimum impedance is required, then the value of the inductance that needs to be introduced in series is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>. (If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>10</mn></msqrt></math>)
question: A telegraph line of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> has a capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and it carries an alternating current at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> kilo cycle per second. If minimum impedance is required, then the value of the inductance that needs to be introduced in series is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>. (If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>10</mn></msqrt></math>)
solution:
The frequency given is 0.5 kilo cycle per second ⇒f=500 Hz⇒ω=2πf=1000π rad s-1.

Total capacitance of the wire is C=0.01×10-6×100 F.

Impedance is minimum at resonance. Therefore, for minimum impedance

XL=XC

⇒ωL=1ωC⇒L=1ω2C=110002×π2×0.01×10-6×100=10-1 H=100 mH

subject: Physics
q: The figure shows a circuit that contains four identical resistors with resistance R=2.0 Ω, two identical inductors with inductance L=2.0 mH and an ideal battery with E.M.F. E=9 V. The current i just after the switch S is closed will be:
question: The figure shows a circuit that contains four identical resistors with resistance R=2.0 Ω, two identical inductors with inductance L=2.0 mH and an ideal battery with E.M.F. E=9 V. The current i just after the switch S is closed will be:
solution:
Just after the switch is closed, inductor will behave like infinite resistance (open circuit) so the circuit will look like

i=9R+R=94=2.25 A

q: For the given figures, choose the correct options:
question: For the given figures, choose the correct options:
options: The rms current in circuit (b) can never be larger than that in (a)
The rms current in figure (a) is always equal to that in figure (b)
The rms current in circuit (b) can be larger than that in (a)
At resonance, current in (b) is less than that in (a)
solution:
For the circuit(a), Z=R=40 Ω. Hence,

Irms=22040=5.5 A

XL=2πfL=2π×50×50×10-3=15.7 Ω and XC=12πfC=12π×50×0.5×10-6=6369 Ω

For the circuit(b), Z=R2+XL-XC2>40 Ω as

XL is not equal to XC. So rms current in (b) can never be larger than (a)

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>, a capacitor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> ac source. If the current is in phase with the emf, the amplitude of current of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is-
question: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>, a capacitor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> ac source. If the current is in phase with the emf, the amplitude of current of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is-
solution:
Here, emf and current are in phase, so the circuit is either pure resistive circuit or case of resonance,

XL=XC

Thus, the impedance of circuit is  Z=R2+XL-XC2=R=20 Ω

Now, rms value of current is irms=22020=11 A

Using the relation between peak and rms value of current, we have peak value i0=irms2=112=242 A.

Hence, the value of x=242.

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure an inductor of inductance 200 mH is connected to an AC source of emf 220 Vand frequency 50 Hz. The instantaneous voltage of the source is 0 V when the peak value of current is aπ A. The value of a is _____ .
question: As shown in the figure an inductor of inductance 200 mH is connected to an AC source of emf 220 Vand frequency 50 Hz. The instantaneous voltage of the source is 0 V when the peak value of current is aπ A. The value of a is _____ .
solution:
The reactance of the given inductor is,

XL=ωL=2πfL=2π×50×0.2=20π Ω.

The maximum current in the given circuit will be,

i0=2VrmsXL=2×22020π=242π A

Therefore, a=242

subject: Physics
q: A series LCR circuit is connected to an ac source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The circuit contain a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and an inductor of inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>79</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The capacitance of the capacitor needed to maximize the average rate at which energy is supplied will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μF</mi></math>.
question: A series LCR circuit is connected to an ac source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The circuit contain a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and an inductor of inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>79</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The capacitance of the capacitor needed to maximize the average rate at which energy is supplied will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μF</mi></math>.
solution:
The average rate at which energy supplied i.e. power delivered is maximum in case of the resonance, therefore

XC=XL

⇒1ωC=79.6 Ω

⇒   C=1ω×79.6=12πf×79.6=1100π×79.6=40  μF

Hence, C=40 μF.

subject: Physics
q: An AC circuit has an inductor and a resistor of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> in series, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. Now, a capacitor is added in series such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. the ratio of the new power factor with the old power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt><mo>:</mo><mi>x</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: An AC circuit has an inductor and a resistor of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> in series, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. Now, a capacitor is added in series such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. the ratio of the new power factor with the old power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt><mo>:</mo><mi>x</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
First Recall the formula of power factor, cosϕ=RZ ⇒cosϕinitial =RZ=RXL2+R2=R9R2+R2=110. 

 ⇒cosϕfinal =RZ=RXL-XC2+R2=RR2+R2=12

⇒cosϕfinal cosϕinitial =5x⇒x=1

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : When the frequency of an AC source in a series LCR circuit increases, the current in the circuit first increases, attains a maximum value and then decreases.

Statement II : In a series LCR circuit, the value of power factor at resonance is one.

In the light of given statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : When the frequency of an AC source in a series LCR circuit increases, the current in the circuit first increases, attains a maximum value and then decreases.

Statement II : In a series LCR circuit, the value of power factor at resonance is one.

In the light of given statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is correct but Statement II is false
solution:
When an a.c. voltage is applied in a series LCR circuit, the current is increased first, attains a maximum and then decreases.

The formula to calculate the power factor is given by

cosϕ=RR2+XL2-XC2   ...1

At resonance, XL=XC, Hence,

cosϕresonance=1

Thus, both the statements are true.

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is charged completely using a de supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. It is now connected to an inductor of inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> to form an LC circuit. The maximum current in LC circuit will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
question: A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is charged completely using a de supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. It is now connected to an inductor of inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> to form an LC circuit. The maximum current in LC circuit will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
solution:
Energy stored in capacitor

E=12CV2=12500×10-6×104

⇒E=52J

Current will be maximum when whole energy of capacitor becomes energy of inductor.

⇒12LI2=52

⇒I=5L=550×10-3=10 A

subject: Physics
q: In a series LCR circuit, the inductance, capacitance and resistance are L=100 mH, C=100 μFand R=10 Ω respectively. They are connected to an AC source of voltage 220 V and frequency of 50 Hz. The approximate value of current in the circuit will be _____ A.
question: In a series LCR circuit, the inductance, capacitance and resistance are L=100 mH, C=100 μFand R=10 Ω respectively. They are connected to an AC source of voltage 220 V and frequency of 50 Hz. The approximate value of current in the circuit will be _____ A.
solution:
Angular frequency, ω=2πf=100π rad s-1

Here, inductive reactance XL=ωL=100π100×10-3=10π Ω

And capacitive reactanceXC=1ωC=1100π×100×10-6=100π Ω=10π Ω

Now, impedance of the series circuit is  Z=R2+XL-XC2=R=10 Ω

Therefore,

IRMS=VRMSZ,

=22010=22 A

subject: Physics
q: A transformer has an efficiency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and works at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the secondary voltage is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the current in the secondary coil is:
question: A transformer has an efficiency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>%</mo></math> and works at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the secondary voltage is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the current in the secondary coil is:
solution:
Efficiency η=PoutputPinput=ESISEPIP

⇒0.8=240IS4000

⇒IS=3200240=13.33 A

subject: Physics
q: The effective current I in the given circuit at very high frequencies will be _____ A.
question: The effective current I in the given circuit at very high frequencies will be _____ A.
solution:
At very high frequencies, inductor acts as an open circuit and capacitor as short circuit.

So, capacitive reactance, XC=1ωC≈0

Also, inductive reactance, XL=ωL≈∞

Thus, equivalent circuit can be redrawn as

Here, both 4 Ω resistances are in parallel and together they are in series with1 Ω and 2 Ω resistances.

Equivalent impedance is Z=1+4×44+4+2=5 Ω

Effective current is I=220 V5 Ω=44 A

subject: Physics
q: A circuit element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> when connected to an AC supply of peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> gives a peak current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> which is in phase with the voltage. A second element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> when connected to the same AC supply also gives the same value of peak current which lags behind the voltage by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> are connected in series to the same supply, what will be the rms value of the current in ampere?
question: A circuit element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> when connected to an AC supply of peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> gives a peak current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> which is in phase with the voltage. A second element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> when connected to the same AC supply also gives the same value of peak current which lags behind the voltage by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> are connected in series to the same supply, what will be the rms value of the current in ampere?
solution:
As current is in phase with the applied voltage, element X should be resistive with R=V0I0=1005=20 Ω.

As current lags behind voltage by 90°, element Y should be inductive with XL=V0I0=1005=20 Ω

When X and Y are connector in series, 

Impedance, Z=XL2+R2=202+202=202 Ω

Now, peak current, I0=V0Z=100202=52 A

Thus, the rms value of current is Irms=I02=52 A

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I with List II.

  List I   List II
(a) Rectifier (i) Used either for stepping up or stepping down the A.C. voltage
(b) Stabilizer (ii) Used to convert A.C. voltage into D.C. voltage
(c) Transformer (iii) Used to remove any ripple in the rectified output voltage
(d) Filter (iv) Used for constant output voltage even when the input voltage or load current change

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List I with List II.

  List I   List II
(a) Rectifier (i) Used either for stepping up or stepping down the A.C. voltage
(b) Stabilizer (ii) Used to convert A.C. voltage into D.C. voltage
(c) Transformer (iii) Used to remove any ripple in the rectified output voltage
(d) Filter (iv) Used for constant output voltage even when the input voltage or load current change

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(a) Rectifier → A.C. to D.C.

(b) Stabilizer → used for constant output voltage even when input voltage or current change.

(c) Transformer → Step- up or step - down A.C. voltage.

(d) Filter → used to remove any ripple in the rectified output voltage.

subject: Physics
q: For a given series LCR circuit it is found that maximum current is drawn when value of variable capacitance is $25 \mathrm{nF}$. If resistance of $200 \Omega$ and $100 \mathrm{mH}$ inductor is being used in the given circuit. The frequency of ac source is ______ $\times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$. (given $\pi^2=10$ )
question: For a given series LCR circuit it is found that maximum current is drawn when value of variable capacitance is $25 \mathrm{nF}$. If resistance of $200 \Omega$ and $100 \mathrm{mH}$ inductor is being used in the given circuit. The frequency of ac source is ______ $\times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$. (given $\pi^2=10$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}_0=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{L} \times \mathrm{C}}} \\ & \mathrm{f}_0=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{100 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \times 10^{-9}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{25 \times 10^{-10}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 5} \times 10^5 \times \sqrt{10} \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & =\frac{100}{10} \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & \mathrm{f}_0=10 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A direct current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and an alternating current of peak value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flow through resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of heat produced in the two resistances in same interval of time will be :
question: A direct current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and an alternating current of peak value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flow through resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of heat produced in the two resistances in same interval of time will be :
solution:
Heat produced in resistor when direct current flows is H1=i2R1t   

H1=423t=163t

Heat produced in resistor when alternating current flows is  H2=irms2R2t                irms=i02  

H2=i022R2t

H2=422×2t=16t

Thus, the ratio is H1:H2=3:1

subject: Physics
q: The amplitude of a damped oscillator decreases to 0.9 times its original magnitude in 5s. In another 10s it will decrease to <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math> times its original magnitude, where <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math> equals :
question: The amplitude of a damped oscillator decreases to 0.9 times its original magnitude in 5s. In another 10s it will decrease to <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math> times its original magnitude, where <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math> equals :
solution:
A=A0 e-bt2m

After 5 second

0.9 A 0 = A 0  e - b 5 2 m    ...... (i)

After 10 more second

A = A 0  e - b 1 5 2 m      ...... (ii)

From (i) and (ii)

A = 0.729 A 0

subject: Physics
q: A series <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> circuit containing an inductor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mH</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> a capacitor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>120</mn><mtext> μ</mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and a resistor <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is driven by an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The energy dissipated in the circuit in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
question: A series <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> circuit containing an inductor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mH</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> a capacitor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>120</mn><mtext> μ</mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and a resistor <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is driven by an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The energy dissipated in the circuit in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
solution:
(Power factor), Pf=cos⁡ϕ=RZ

Where R, Z are the resistance and impedance respectively.
(Energy dissipated) E=Vrms2Z×cosϕ×t
⇒E=Vrms2Z×RZ×t

⇒E=Vrms2Z2×R×t

Vrms, t are the RMS voltage and time respectively.

We know that the Impedance of a LCR circuit is given by the formula,
     Z2=R2+ωL-1ωC2⇒Z2=602+2π×50×20×10-3-12π×50×120×10-62

Where ω, L, C are the angular frequency, inductance and capacitance in the circuit respectively.
⇒Z2=602+2π-100012π2

⇒Z2=4009.7

∴E=2424009.7×60×60 J 

= 517.14 J

=5.17×102 J

subject: Physics
q: An inductor $(L=100 \mathrm{mH})$, a resistor $(R=100 \Omega)$ and a battery $(E=100 \mathrm{~V})$ are initially connected in series as shown in the figure. After a long time the battery is disconnected after short circuiting the points $A$ and $B$. The current in the circuit $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ after the circuit is
question: An inductor $(L=100 \mathrm{mH})$, a resistor $(R=100 \Omega)$ and a battery $(E=100 \mathrm{~V})$ are initially connected in series as shown in the figure. After a long time the battery is disconnected after short circuiting the points $A$ and $B$. The current in the circuit $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ after the circuit is
solution: $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{Rt} / \mathrm{L}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{A}$

subject: Physics
q: An alternating voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>220</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>100</mn><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> volt is applied to a purely resistive load of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math>. The time taken for the current to rise from half of the peak value to the peak value is:
question: An alternating voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>220</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>100</mn><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> volt is applied to a purely resistive load of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math>. The time taken for the current to rise from half of the peak value to the peak value is:
solution:
Time taken to change current value from half of the peak value to the peak value is, 

t=T6

⇒t=2π6ω=π3ω=π300π=1300=3.33 ms
